abstract: This volume offers a comparative analysis of the political, military, and economic dimensions of Soviet occupation in Austria, Hungary, and Romania. Drawing on extensive evidence from Austrian, Hungarian, Romanian, and Russian archives, the authors challenge prevailing interpretations of Soviet foreign policy. While traditional scholarship has primarily investigated the political motivations behind Stalin’s decision to impose Bolshevik systems within the Soviet sphere of influence, this book argues for a broader understanding of his objectives. It demonstrates that the conquest of East-Central Europe was fundamentally imperial in nature, transforming occupied territories into strategic military and economic assets for the Soviet Union. Finally, the study examines the harrowing human cost of this era, documenting the widespread atrocities, plunder, and forced deportations that defined the daily reality of those living under Soviet rule.
